message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5992806/
**Title:** Age, Hearing, and the Perceptual Learning of Rapid Speech (Manheim, Lavie & Banai, 2018, Ear & Hearing)
**Found via:** Training/plasticity in older adults & blindness onset (early- vs late-blind ultrafast speech) search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt:
Extract all findings relevant to: how AGE and HEARING affect comprehension and perceptual learning (training) of time-compressed/rapid speech. I need:
1. Quantitative results: how do older vs younger adults differ in baseline comprehension of time-compressed speech? Give time-compression ratios, syllables/sec, words/min, % correct.
2. Training/plasticity: can older adults improve via training? By how much? How does their learning compare to younger adults (rate, magnitude, retention)?
3. The role of peripheral hearing loss vs central/cognitive factors — is hearing loss controlled for? Does the age effect persist after controlling for audibility/hearing?
4. Peak age / age bands studied (mean ages, ranges).
5. Any effect sizes, p-values, correlation coefficients.
6. Publication date and study design (sample size, ages).
Quote exact sentences with numbers where possible.

content.content:
# Age and Hearing Effects on Time-Compressed Speech Perception: Key Findings

**Publication:** Manheim et al., 2018, *Trends in Hearing*  
**Sample:** 149 participants (YA n=57, mean age 27±4; ONH n=56, mean age 70±5; OHI n=36, mean age 72±7)

## 1. Baseline Comprehension of Time-Compressed Speech

**Compression ratios used:**
- Young adults (YA): 30% of natural duration
- Older normal hearing (ONH): 35–40% of natural duration  
- Older with hearing loss (OHI): 40% of natural duration

**Performance equivalence achieved:** "Baseline performance on time-compressed speech was similar in the three groups" (*p* = .27), ranging 36–44% correct. However, for natural-fast speech (166 words/min), older groups performed significantly worse: YA 66±13% correct vs. ONH 43±19% vs. OHI 34±15% (*p* < .001).

## 2. Early Learning (Rapid Adaptation)

Age and hearing loss both impaired early learning over 20 sentences:

- **YA:** 88% improved; median improvement 30%
- **ONH:** 77% improved; ~50% improved >10%
- **OHI:** 61% improved (binomial *p* = .24, not significant)

**Statistical comparison:** Kruskal–Wallis χ²(2) = 24.13, *p* < .001. "The magnitude of learning was nevertheless influenced by both age and hearing loss," with mean ranks 95 (YA), 70 (ONH), 51 (OHI). Mann–Whitney contrasts: YA vs. ONH (*Z* = −3.06, *p* = .002); ONH vs. OHI (*Z* = −2.08, *p* = .037).

Early learning predicted natural-fast speech recognition: unique variance contributions 25% (YA), 22% (ONH), 14% (OHI), all *p* < .001.

## 3. Training/Plasticity Over Multi-Session Learning

**Training protocol:** 300 trials, 5 blocks of 60, adaptive staircase (2-down/1-up), semantic verification task, 40–55 minutes.

**Learning during training:** All groups improved verification thresholds (compression rates). No significant age effect on training slope (Kruskal–Wallis *p* = .003 driven by hearing effect: OHI steeper slopes than ONH, Mann–Whitney *Z* = −3.02, *p* = .003).

**Pretest-to-posttest gains (trained vs. control):**

Training × Group interaction significant (*F* = 8.6, *p* < .001). Trained YA showed larger gains on repeated time-compressed sentences than ONH and OHI (age effect: *F* = 8.4, *p* = .005 for age × training interaction). Transfer to untrained time-compressed sentences: YA transferred; both older groups showed "transfer to untrained items was altogether absent."

No additional hearing loss effect on training gains beyond age effect (*p* = .40–.96 for hearing main effects and interactions).

## 4. Age and Peripheral Hearing Loss: Dissociation

**Hearing status controlled:** Baseline time-compressed performance was equated across groups using different compression rates, then compared in trained vs. control designs.

**Age-independent finding:** "Transfer of learning to natural-fast speech occurred regardless of age and hearing…limited to sentences encountered during training."

**Age-dependent finding:** Early learning and transfer to novel time-compressed sentences declined with age (independent of hearing loss). Hearing loss affected early learning additively (*p* = .037 ONH vs. OHI), but "none of the effects that involved hearing status was significant" for training and transfer to natural-fast speech (*p* = .40–.96).

**Interpretation:** "Age-related declines in learning may stem from age differences in the use of high- and low-level speech cues" rather than audibility alone.

## 5. Age Bands and Ranges

- YA: 20–38 years, mean 27±4
- ONH: 65–86 years, mean 70±5  
- OHI: 65–91 years, mean 72±7

## 6. Effect Sizes and Statistical Values

| Measure | χ²/F | *p* | η² or r |
|---------|------|-----|---------|
| Early learning (Kruskal–Wallis) | 24.13 | <.001 | — |
| Pretest natural-fast (Kruskal–Wallis) | 65.88 | <.001 | — |
| Time-compressed learning (ANOVA omnibus) | — | — | 0.478 (Training) |
| Early learning → natural-fast (YA correlation) | — | <.001 | *r* = .58 |
| Early learning → natural-fast (ONH correlation) | — | <.001 | *r* = .76 |
| Early learning → natural-fast (OHI correlation) | — | <.001 | *r* = .78 |

---

## Summary

Older adults show "weaker early learning" and "reduced transfer to untrained time-compressed sentences" despite equated baseline audibility and substantial training-induced improvements. Age effects persist independently of peripheral hearing loss, consistent with central/cognitive mechanisms (reliance on high- vs. low-level speech cues).

content.text: I have comprehensive findings. Note the title in the prompt says "Ear & Hearing" but the actual journal is "Trends in Hearing" — the study and authors match. Let me extract the falsifiable claims.

claims.claim: Older adults (mean ages 70-72) showed weaker rapid early learning of time-compressed speech than young adults (mean age 27): 88% of YA improved (median +30%) vs 77% of older normal-hearing and only 61% of older hearing-impaired, with the group difference highly significant (Kruskal-Wallis chi-square(2)=24.13, p<.001) and persisting after baseline audibility was equated.
claims.quote: The magnitude of learning was nevertheless influenced by both age and hearing loss, with mean ranks 95 (YA), 70 (ONH), 51 (OHI). Mann-Whitney contrasts: YA vs. ONH (Z = -3.06, p = .002); ONH vs. OHI (Z = -2.08, p = .037).

claims.claim: Older adults can still improve their time-compressed speech comprehension through training, but their gains do not generalize: young adults transferred learning to untrained time-compressed sentences whereas in both older groups transfer to untrained items was completely absent (age x training interaction F=8.4, p=.005).
claims.quote: Trained YA showed larger gains on repeated time-compressed sentences than ONH and OHI (age effect: F = 8.4, p = .005 for age x training interaction). Transfer to untrained time-compressed sentences: YA transferred; both older groups showed "transfer to untrained items was altogether absent."

claims.claim: The age-related decline in learning rapid speech is dissociable from peripheral hearing loss: after equating baseline audibility, age effects on early learning and transfer persisted independently, while hearing status had no significant effect on training and transfer to natural-fast speech (p=.40 to .96), implicating central/cognitive rather than purely audibility mechanisms.
claims.quote: none of the effects that involved hearing status was significant" for training and transfer to natural-fast speech (p = .40-.96)... Age-related declines in learning may stem from age differences in the use of high- and low-level speech cues

claims.claim: When baseline time-compressed performance was equated across age groups using different compression rates (YA at 30% of natural duration, older normal-hearing at 35-40%, older hearing-impaired at 40%), baseline percent-correct was statistically similar across all three groups (p=.27, range 36-44% correct), demonstrating older adults need less aggressive compression to match young-adult performance.
claims.quote: Baseline performance on time-compressed speech was similar in the three groups" (p = .27), ranging 36-44% correct.

claims.claim: On natural fast speech (166 words/min, not artificially compressed), comprehension declined sharply with age: young adults scored 66+/-13% correct versus 43+/-19% for older normal-hearing and 34+/-15% for older hearing-impaired adults (p<.001), showing a steep age penalty in real-world fast-speech understanding.
claims.quote: for natural-fast speech (166 words/min), older groups performed significantly worse: YA 66+/-13% correct vs. ONH 43+/-19% vs. OHI 34+/-15% (p < .001).

content.text: Extracted findings from Manheim, Lavie & Banai (2018) — a primary peer-reviewed study (PMC5992806). Note: the orchestrator's metadata labels the journal "Ear & Hearing" but the article itself is published in *Trends in Hearing*; authors, year, and topic otherwise match. Key relevance to the research question: it directly addresses trainability/plasticity across age (point 5) and the peripheral-vs-central mechanism dissociation (point 3), with a strong design (N=149, baseline audibility equated across YA mean 27, older normal-hearing mean 70, older hearing-impaired mean 72). It does not cover blindness onset or the childhood developmental curve.
